context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Global Poverty

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How has development come to be associated popularly with poverty alleviation? What do we make of techniques of poverty alleviation such as microfinance, in terms of the theories of economic development? Is it possible to focus on poverty successfully in a global economy characterized by hyper-capitalism? Are the techniques for measuring poverty, and the goal-setting exercised like MDG, satisfactory?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I need help figuring out how to structure my 700–800 word reflection on global poverty. I’m kinda stuck on how to connect poverty alleviation stuff like microfinance to bigger development theories.
exchanges.tutor:
Good starting point! Before we think about structure, let's dig into the ideas themselves — structure will follow naturally once your thinking is clear.

Let's start with the first question in the prompt: why do you think development became so closely associated with poverty alleviation in the popular imagination? What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for structural/organizational help rather than engaging with the substantive questions of the prompt. Before I help with structure, I should redirect them to the content itself, since structure will follow from their thinking. I'll use a Socratic opener to get them engaging with the actual ideas first, specifically the first question in the prompt about how development became associated with poverty alleviation. This is the logical starting point and will naturally lead into the microfinance/theory connection they mentioned.

exchanges.student: I guess cuz when ppl hear “development,” they think of poor countries needing help, aid campaigns, NGOs, that kinda thing. But idk if that’s the whole story or just the popular/media version.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp instinct — you're already sensing there's a gap between the popular image and something more complex. So what do you think came first historically: was development *always* about poverty, or did it start somewhere else? Think about what you've read in the course.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a useful distinction between the popular/media version and a potentially deeper story. This is good intuition and worth reinforcing, but I should push them to think about what the 'deeper story' actually is — specifically, the historical and theoretical context of how development thinking evolved. This connects to the course themes about history of development planning and the role of external actors. I'll ask them to think about where development thinking came from historically to push them toward a more nuanced answer.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it started only w poverty tbh. I think it was more about modernization, economic growth, and state-building first, then poverty became the more visible moral framing later.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong observation — and it's central to the prompt. So what do you think drove that shift toward poverty as the dominant moral framing? What forces or actors might have pushed development thinking in that direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely good insight — the historical shift from modernization/growth paradigms to poverty-focused framing. This is a key idea worth acknowledging. Now I should push them to think about *why* that shift happened, which will naturally lead them toward the microfinance/techniques question and the tension between poverty alleviation and broader development theories. A single focused question will keep momentum.

exchanges.student: Maybe donors, intl orgs, and NGOs pushed it cuz “reduce poverty” sounds measurable and morally urgent. Also maybe after big growth-focused plans didn’t fix inequality, the focus shifted more to poor ppl directly?
exchanges.tutor: Both points are solid. Now connect that to microfinance: given what you just said about shifting from growth theories to direct poverty targeting, where does something like microfinance sit theoretically? Does it challenge or reinforce mainstream development thinking?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making two good points: the role of international actors in shaping the poverty framing, and the failure of growth-focused approaches to address inequality. Both connect well to course themes. Now I should bridge this to the next part of the prompt — microfinance as a technique — and ask them to think about how microfinance fits (or doesn't fit) within broader development theories. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: I’m kinda torn ngl — microfinance feels pro-poor, but also super market-based. So maybe it looks like poverty help on the surface, while still reinforcing neoliberal/mainstream ideas like individual entrepreneurship over structural change.
exchanges.tutor: That tension is exactly the right thing to be wrestling with. So does that bring you to a conclusion about the third question in the prompt — can poverty alleviation actually succeed in a hyper-capitalist global economy, or does that tension you just identified make it structurally limited?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has landed on a genuinely sophisticated tension that is central to the prompt. This is excellent thinking and I should acknowledge it briefly, then push them toward the third prompt question about whether poverty focus can succeed under hyper-capitalism. This will deepen their critique and help them build a coherent argument across the reflection.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think that makes it pretty limited tbh. Like it can maybe ease some symptoms, but if the global economy keeps producing inequality, then poverty-focused fixes prob won’t solve the deeper causes.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a coherent thread building through your argument. Now what about the last part of the prompt: do you think tools like the MDGs and standard poverty measurement techniques are adequate? Do they fit with the critique you've been developing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is developing a coherent critical argument across the first three prompt questions. Now I should move them to the final question about measurement techniques and MDGs, which they haven't addressed yet. This is an important component of the prompt that needs to be in their reflection. I'll ask a focused question to get them thinking about it.

exchanges.student: I’d say only partly cuz they make poverty legible and easier to target, but they reduce it to indicators and income thresholds. That kinda fits my critique since measurement can hide power, inequality, and the political causes of poverty.
exchanges.tutor: You've now developed a clear line of argument across all four parts of the prompt. That's solid foundation work. Why don't you try writing a draft of your reflection now, and come back if you get stuck? The thread connecting your ideas seems pretty clear — trust it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now addressed all four components of the prompt with substantive ideas. We've had about 6 exchanges and they have enough material to write. Per the rules about sensing redundancy and integrating discussion into written content, this is a good moment to ask them to move toward writing. I should acknowledge their progress and push them to start drafting rather than continuing to discuss.

exchanges.student: Can u help me make a quick outline first? I just want smth simple so I don’t ramble.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually something you can do yourself based on what we just discussed — you already have the key ideas in order. What would your four main points be if you had to list them right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to make an outline for them, which would mean doing the organizational work on their behalf. Per the rules, I should not complete tasks for the student. However, I can ask them to try building the outline themselves based on what they've already figured out in our conversation, which is a reasonable scaffold without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I think: development wasn’t originally just about poverty, then poverty became the public/moral frame, microfinance shows the market-based tension, and MDGs/poverty metrics are useful but limited. Is that too basic or is that enough for a reflection?
exchanges.tutor: Those four points cover all the prompt questions, which is good. But ask yourself: is there one overarching argument connecting all four, or are they just sitting next to each other? A strong reflection usually has a central claim it keeps returning to.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid outline on their own. I should not grade it but can give general evaluative feedback. The outline covers all four prompt questions which is good, but it's somewhat descriptive/linear — I can push them to think about whether there's a stronger argumentative thread connecting all four points, without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe my main claim is that poverty became the face of development b/c it’s morally compelling and measurable, but that focus can depoliticize development and fit too easily into neoliberal capitalism. Does that sound focused enough?
exchanges.tutor: That's a focused and substantive central claim — it has real argumentative force. You're ready to write. Go draft it and come back if you hit a wall.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong central thesis that ties together all four prompt components. This is a genuine breakthrough moment. I should acknowledge it positively but briefly, and now firmly push them to start writing since we're near the end of the planned conversation length and they have everything they need.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic session throughout all ten exchanges. Key ideas were consistently drawn from the student rather than supplied by the tutor; the student generated the modernization-to-poverty-framing shift, the neoliberal tension in microfinance, and the depoliticization critique entirely through their own reasoning. The tutor asked focused follow-up questions, built on each student response, maintained bite-sized turns, and appropriately deflected the outline request back to the student. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the session stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never supplied claims, evidence, or reasoning on behalf of the student. Every substantive idea — the historical shift from modernization to poverty framing, the neoliberal critique of microfinance, the depoliticization argument, the MDG critique — was elicited through questioning and confirmed/extended by the student. No near-submission-ready output was produced. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on each student response before advancing to the next prompt question, and there was no evidence of misdiagnosed confusion. 1.3: When the student's outline was slightly underdeveloped (Turn 9), the tutor gave specific process-level feedback about needing a unifying central claim rather than just listing points. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed without repetition or drift; all exchanges remained anchored to the four prompt questions. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-phrased, and consistently supportive without being evaluative or grade-like.
